Notes on Accessible Color Systems

June 17, 2026 · darshanaswathappa

Building accessible color systems that meet WCAG AA contrast requirements is harder than it looks. Most design systems specify a set of gray tones that look refined on a large monitor in a well-lit room. However, those same tones often fail contrast checks for users with low vision, or on screens with lower brightness settings. Our research notes track explored what happens when you audit a real design system and have to fix it without losing the brand.

The Problem With Muted Text Colors

The most common failure we find in design systems is muted text. A typical pattern is to use a mid-gray — something like #808080 — for secondary or helper text. That color achieves a contrast ratio of roughly 3.9:1 against white. WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 for normal text. So a color that looks acceptable to designers with standard vision fails for a significant portion of users.

Furthermore, the failure compounds. When you use that muted gray on a card background that is itself a light gray rather than white, the ratio drops further. A 3.9:1 ratio can become 3.2:1 almost invisibly. This is why accessible color systems must be tested in context, not in isolation.

Our Accessible Color Systems WCAG AA Audit Process

We developed a three-step process that any team can repeat. First, export every text color token and its intended background context. Second, run each pair through a contrast checker — we used the WCAG 2.1 minimum contrast guidance as our reference. Third, for every failing pair, find the darkest version of that color that still reads as the same hue before resorting to a new token entirely.

In our case, the muted gray moved from #808080 to #595959. That is a meaningful step darker, but it still reads as secondary text rather than primary text. The contrast ratio at #595959 against white is 7:1, which exceeds WCAG AA and meets WCAG AAA. The brand does not break; the text simply becomes readable.

What We Learned About Accessible Color Systems

The key insight is that WCAG AA is a floor, not a ceiling. In addition, testing colors in isolation is not enough — always test them in their actual context. A color that passes at 4.6:1 on white may fail at 3.8:1 on your card background. The fix is never to pick an arbitrary darker shade; it is to understand the actual backgrounds the color will appear against and set the token accordingly.

We also learned that accessible color decisions are easier to make early and much harder to retrofit. Therefore, we now include a contrast check as a required step in the design review process for every new component the program builds.

Read more about our research on the research blog, or learn about the program on the About page.

Tagged

  • #accessibility
  • #design systems
